topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara found the box tucked deep in the attic, a dusty cardboard container smelling faintly of lavender and old paper. Inside, meticulously organized in clear plastic sleeves, were every single assignment her mother had ever completed—from kindergarten reports to university theses. As Elara ran a finger over a pristine essay on the French Revolution, a strange tightness constricted her chest. She shifted her weight, the floorboards creaking under her bare feet, and quickly snapped the lid shut. The silence of the attic seemed amplified, pressing in on her. She looked around the cluttered space, suddenly hyper-aware of every shadow stretching across the rafters. A cold prickle traced its way down her spine, making the fine hairs on her arms stand up. She didn't want to touch anything else in that box.

[story 2]

"Mom, what is all this?" Liam asked, holding up a stack of meticulously graded math worksheets. His mother, usually so cheerful, was standing by the hall closet, her expression carefully neutral. "These are... all your schoolwork?" She offered a tight, practiced smile that didn't reach her eyes. Liam felt a sudden, unwelcome jolt of apprehension. He noticed the way she avoided his gaze, her hands clasped tightly in front of her. When he pointed to a binder labeled 'Grade 10 History,' she flinched almost imperceptibly, a tiny tremor running through her shoulders. The air in the hallway felt suddenly thick, heavy, as if something unseen was watching them from the darkened corners of the room.

[story 3]

I traced the edge of the ledger, the paper cool beneath my fingertips. It was a collection of every test, every project, every piece of homework my mother had ever managed. A peculiar sense of wrongness settled over me, a heavy cloak draped over my shoulders. I tried to focus on the neat handwriting, the perfectly executed diagrams, but my attention kept drifting to the corners of the room. Every time I glanced toward the window, I caught a fleeting impression of movement, a shape that dissolved before I could properly identify it. I swallowed hard, my throat suddenly dry. The quiet of the room was no longer peaceful; it was expectant, waiting for a sound that never came.
